**Setup:** 13B model on H100, static batching, 2048 prompt tokens, 2048 completion tokens.

**Step 1: Prefill time per sequence.**
FLOPs per prefill = 2 × 13×10^9 × 2048 = 53.25 TFLOPs.
H100 FP16 peak = 989 TFLOPS. At ~50% MFU for prefill: effective = 494.5 TFLOPS.
Prefill time = 53.25×10^12 / 494.5×10^12 ≈ 108 ms per sequence.
(At 100% utilization: 53.25/989 = 53.8 ms — use this as lower bound.)

**Step 2: Decode time per token (memory-bound, batch=1).**
Model weights in FP16 = 13×10^9 × 2 bytes = 26 GB.
H100 HBM3 bandwidth = 3.35 TB/s.
Per-token decode latency = 26 GB / 3.35 TB/s = 7.76 ms/token.

**Step 3: Total decode time for 2048 output tokens.**
Decode = 2048 × 7.76 ms = 15.9 seconds per sequence.

**Step 4: Static batch processing time at batch size B.**
T_batch(B) = B × 108 ms (prefill, sequential) + 2048 × 7.76 ms (decode, parallel across batch).
For B=1: T_batch = 108 ms + 15,900 ms ≈ 16.0 seconds.

**Step 5: P99 TTFT analysis.**
In static batching, a new request arriving must wait for the current batch to complete before its prefill begins.
P99 TTFT ≈ T_wait(worst case) + T_own_prefill.
Worst case: request arrives just as previous batch starts → wait = T_batch(B).
For B=1: P99 TTFT = 16,000 ms + 108 ms ≈ 16.1 seconds >> 400 ms SLO.

**Step 6: Conclusion.**
Even at B=1, the P99 TTFT is ~16 seconds because the long decode phase (15.9s) of the previous batch blocks new requests. The 400 ms SLO requires T_batch < 400 ms, but decode alone takes 15.9s. Static batching is fundamentally incompatible with strict TTFT SLOs when completions are long. Continuous batching decouples prefill scheduling from decode, allowing new requests to begin prefill while other requests are mid-decode, achieving TTFT ≈ prefill time ≈ 108 ms.
fixes.realistic_solution: The P99 TTFT is dominated by waiting for the previous batch's decode phase to complete. For a 13B model on H100 at batch=1: model weights = 26 GB (FP16), decode = 26 GB / 3.35 TB/s = 7.76 ms/token, so 2048 tokens of decode = 15.9 seconds. Prefill = 2 × 13B × 2048 / 989 TFLOPS ≈ 54-108 ms depending on utilization. Total batch time ≈ 16 seconds. Even at B=1, a worst-case arriving request waits 16 seconds — 40× the 400 ms SLO. Static batching fundamentally fails because the long decode phase blocks all new prefills. Continuous batching solves this by interleaving prefill of new requests between decode iterations (~7.76 ms apart), achieving P99 TTFT ≈ prefill time + one decode step ≈ 116 ms.

A single report prefill blocks the GPU for 14-28 seconds.
At 100 real-time requests/second arrival rate, during one report prefill:

All 1,420 requests violate their 500 ms deadline.

**4. Capacity planning for real-time tier:**
Each real-time request needs ~71 ms of GPU time (prefill) + decode.

Per-token decode = 140 GB / 3.35 TB/s = 41.8 ms/token.
Total per-request time = 71 ms + 200 × 41.8 ms = 8.43 seconds.

**Setup:** 70B model on H100, sequential request processing.

**Time per decode step (memory-bound):**
Model weights FP16 = 70×10^9 × 2 = 140 GB.
H100 HBM3 bandwidth = 3.35 TB/s.
Decode latency per token = 140 GB / 3.35 TB/s = 41.8 ms/token.

**Prefill time for 100-token prompt:**
FLOPs = 2 × 70×10^9 × 100 = 14 TFLOPs.
H100 FP16 peak = 989 TFLOPS.
Prefill time = 14×10^12 / 989×10^12 = 14.2 ms.
(Note: short prompts are still compute-bound — 14.2 ms is significant, not negligible.)

**Per-request time (100 prompt + 100 output tokens):**
Prefill: 14.2 ms.
Decode: 100 × 41.8 ms = 4,180 ms ≈ 4.2 seconds.
Total per request ≈ 4.2 seconds.

**Head-of-line blocking with 20 queued requests:**
Sequential processing: each request takes ~4.2s.
A new request must wait for all ahead of it.
Worst-case wait = 19 × 4.2s = 79.8 seconds TTFT >> 500 ms SLO.
Even with 5 ahead: 5 × 4.2s = 21 seconds TTFT.

**With continuous batching (batch size B):**
All B requests decode in parallel; each decode step still takes ~41.8 ms (memory-bound, weight load is shared).
Each step produces B tokens (one per request).
TTFT for a new request ≈ 1 decode step wait + prefill = 41.8 ms + 14.2 ms ≈ 56 ms.
Throughput: B tokens / 41.8 ms → for B=20: 20/0.0418 ≈ 478 tokens/s.
P99 TTFT now dominated by prefill of the new request (~14 ms), not queue depth.

70B model in FP16 = 140 GB weights. Each decode token loads all weights from HBM once.

**H100 (80 GB HBM3):**
Model requires TP=2 minimum (140 GB > 80 GB). 70 GB/GPU.
Bandwidth per GPU = 3.35 TB/s. Per-token decode = 70 GB / 3.35 TB/s = 20.9 ms/token.
With NVLink AllReduce overhead (~0.02 ms): total ≈ 20.9 ms/token → ~47.8 tokens/s.
Cost ≈ 2 × $3.50/hr = $7.00/hr.

Bandwidth per GPU = 2.0 TB/s. Per-token decode = 70 GB / 2.0 TB/s = 35.0 ms/token.
With NVLink overhead: ~35 ms/token → ~28.6 tokens/s.
Cost ≈ 2 × $2.20/hr = $4.40/hr.
Tokens/hr = 28.6 × 3600 = 102,857.
Cost per 1M tokens = $4.40 / 102,857 × 10^6 = **$42.78/M tokens**.

Cost per 1M tokens = $10.80 / 189,360 × 10^6 = **$57.03/M tokens** (at batch=1).

H100: 989 TFLOPS FP16. FLOPs/token = 2 × 70B = 140 GFLOPs.

**Verdict:** At batch=1 (decode-bound), H100 wins on $/token due to superior bandwidth per dollar. At high batch sizes (compute-bound), both H100 and TPU v5e converge to similar $/token. Let B=8, S=2048, H=4096, d_h=128, heads=32. Tokens = B × S = 16,384.

**(1) QKV projection** ([BS × H] × [H × 3H]): 2 × 16,384 × 4,096 × 12,288 = 1.649 TFLOPs.

**(2) Attention scores — two parts:**
(a) QK^T: For each head, [BS × d_h] × [d_h × S] per batch element.
Per head, per batch: 2 × S × S × d_h = 2 × 2048 × 2048 × 128.
All heads, all batches: 32 × 8 × 2 × 2048 × 2048 × 128 = 274.9 GFLOPs.
(b) Attn × V: Same shape as QK^T → another 274.9 GFLOPs.
**Total attention: 274.9 + 274.9 = 549.8 GFLOPs ≈ 0.55 TFLOPs.**

**(3) Output projection** ([BS × H] × [H × H]): 2 × 16,384 × 4,096 × 4,096 = 549.8 GFLOPs ≈ 0.55 TFLOPs.

**(4) MLP** (standard 4× expansion: up-project H→4H, down-project 4H→H):
Up: 2 × 16,384 × 4,096 × 16,384 = 2.199 TFLOPs.
Down: 2 × 16,384 × 16,384 × 4,096 = 2.199 TFLOPs.
**Total MLP: 2.199 + 2.199 = 4.398 TFLOPs.**

**Total per layer: 1.649 + 0.55 + 0.55 + 4.398 ≈ 7.15 TFLOPs.**
MLP is 4.398/7.15 = **61.5%** of compute.
Attention (QK^T + AV) is 0.55/7.15 = **7.7%** of compute.
Projections (QKV + Output) are 2.199/7.15 = **30.8%** of compute.

For 32 layers: 7.15 × 32 = 228.8 TFLOPs.
On H100 at 989 TFLOPS: 228.8 / 989 = **231 ms** minimum (compute-bound).
On A100 at 312 TFLOPS: 228.8 / 312 = **733 ms** minimum.

The MLP dominates the FLOP count. The O(S²) attention only becomes dominant when S exceeds ~8K for this architecture.

**Assumptions:** H100 cluster, 8 GPUs, single FIFO queue. Standard jobs: 30s processing each, arriving at 20 jobs/min during launch surge. Premium jobs: 200ms processing, P99 TTFT < 200ms.

**During product launch surge:**
Arrival rate = 20 Standard jobs/min = 0.33 jobs/s.
Service rate = 8 GPUs / 30s per job = 0.267 jobs/s.
Since arrival (0.33) > service (0.267), queue grows unboundedly.
After 5 minutes: backlog ≈ (0.33 − 0.267) × 300s × 30s = excess of ~19 Standard jobs queued.
With 50 Standard jobs queued (realistic after ~25 min of surge):
Wait time for new request = 50 × 30s / 8 GPUs = **187.5 seconds**.
This is **937× the 200ms SLA**.

**Adding 8 more GPUs (playbook action):**
Service rate = 16 GPUs / 30s = 0.533 jobs/s > 0.33 arrival rate → queue drains.
But during transient: 50 × 30s / 16 = **93.75 seconds** — still 468× the SLA.
And critically, adding GPUs only helps if the surge is temporary. If the Standard arrival rate increases further (as launches often do), the problem returns.

**Why the playbook is fundamentally wrong:**
Even with sufficient capacity, FIFO ordering means Premium requests sit behind Standard jobs. If 10 Standard jobs are ahead of a Premium request: wait = 10 × 30s / 16 = 18.75 seconds. Still 93× the SLA.

**With priority queuing:**
Premium requests jump to front. Premium wait = (0 Standard ahead) + (at most 2 Premium ahead × 0.2s) / 8 GPUs = **0.05 seconds = 50 ms** — within the 200ms SLA with zero additional hardware.
fixes.realistic_solution: The playbook is wrong because this is a scheduling problem, not a capacity problem. The FIFO queue causes Head-of-Line blocking: high-priority Premium requests (200ms) are stuck behind low-priority Standard jobs (30s each). With 50 queued Standard jobs and 8 GPUs, Premium waits 187.5 seconds (937× SLA). Adding GPUs reduces transient wait but doesn't prevent Premium requests from queueing behind Standard ones. The fix: multi-level priority queue where Premium always preempts Standard, achieving ~50ms wait. This is architectural, not capacity-based.

fixes.napkin_math:
**H100 specs:** 989 TFLOPS FP16, 3.35 TB/s HBM3 bandwidth. Ridge point = 989,000 / 3,350 = **295 FLOPs/byte**.
**B200 specs (approximate):** ~4,500 TFLOPS FP16 (~4.5× H100), ~8 TB/s bandwidth (~2.4× H100). Ridge point = 4,500,000 / 8,000 = **562 FLOPs/byte**.

**Large LLM (70B, batch=64 prefill):**
Arithmetic intensity ≈ batch × 2 FLOPs per weight / 2 bytes per weight = 64 FLOPs/byte.
On H100: 64 < 295, memory-bound. Throughput = 3.35 TB/s × 64 FLOPs/byte = 214 TFLOPS.
On B200: 64 < 562, still memory-bound. Throughput = 8 TB/s × 64 FLOPs/byte = 512 TFLOPS.
**Speedup: 512/214 = 2.4×** — tracks bandwidth ratio, not compute ratio. Good ROI if B200 costs ≤ 2.4× H100.

**MobileNet (batch=1 inference):**
~300M FLOPs, ~13 MB weights. AI = 300M / 13M = **23 FLOPs/byte**.
On H100: 23 < 295, memory-bound. Throughput = 3.35 TB/s × 23 = 77 TFLOPS.
On B200: 23 < 562, still memory-bound. Throughput = 8 TB/s × 23 = 184 TFLOPS.
**Speedup: 184/77 = 2.4×** — identical to LLM case because both are memory-bound.

**The paradox:**
Both workloads get the same 2.4× speedup (set by bandwidth ratio). But the B200 costs ~3× more per hour.
Cost-efficiency for MobileNet: 2.4× speedup at 3× cost = **0.8× the H100's cost-efficiency** — you pay more per inference.
The B200 only wins on price-performance for workloads above its ridge point (562 FLOPs/byte), e.g., large-batch training with AI > 562. For memory-bound workloads, the B200's extra 4.5× compute is entirely stranded.

**1. Workload Arithmetic Intensity (AI) for batch-1 LLM decode:**
Each token: read full weight matrix, do one multiply-accumulate per weight.
AI = 2 FLOPs / 2 bytes (FP16 weight) = **1 FLOP/Byte**. Extremely low.

**2. Ridge Points:**
- **B200:** 2,250 TFLOPS / 8.0 TB/s = **281 FLOPs/Byte**.
- **Gracewing:** 1,500 TFLOPS / 12.0 TB/s = **125 FLOPs/Byte**.
At AI = 1, both are deeply memory-bandwidth-bound.

**3. Actual throughput (bandwidth-limited):**
- **B200:** 8.0 TB/s × 1 FLOP/Byte = 8.0 TFLOPS effective. Utilization = 8.0 / 2,250 = **0.36%** of peak.
- **Gracewing:** 12.0 TB/s × 1 FLOP/Byte = 12.0 TFLOPS effective. Utilization = 12.0 / 1,500 = **0.80%** of peak.

**4. Tokens/s per chip (batch=1 decode, 70B FP16 = 140 GB weights):**
- **B200:** 140 GB / 8.0 TB/s = **17.5 ms/token → 57.1 tokens/s**. Power: 1,000W. Efficiency: **0.057 tokens/s/W**.
- **Gracewing:** 140 GB / 12.0 TB/s = **11.7 ms/token → 85.5 tokens/s**. Power: 800W. Efficiency: **0.107 tokens/s/W**.

**5. Fleet sizing under 50 MW power cap:**
- **B200:** 50,000,000W / 1,000W = 50,000 chips × 57.1 tok/s = **2.86M tokens/s**.
- **Gracewing:** 50,000,000W / 800W = 62,500 chips × 85.5 tok/s = **5.34M tokens/s**.

**6. Conclusion:** Gracewing delivers **1.87×** the throughput under the same power budget. Its higher memory bandwidth (12 vs 8 TB/s) and lower TDP (800 vs 1,000W) are the only metrics that matter for memory-bound batch-1 inference. The B200's extra compute is entirely wasted — 99.64% of its TFLOPS sit idle.

H100: 80 GB HBM3, 3.35 TB/s bandwidth. All models FP16.

Fits on 1 GPU. Free HBM = 80 − 14 = 66 GB.

H100: 80 GB HBM3, 3.35 TB/s bandwidth, 989 TFLOPS FP16.
Free HBM = 80 − 14 = 66 GB.

Each step produces 1 token per request → 239 tokens/s per request.

16 GB HBM per chip, 819 GB/s bandwidth.

Must shard TP=2: 7 GB/chip, 9 GB free.

H100 at batch=61: $0.067/M tokens. TPU v5e at batch=8: $0.71/M tokens.
H100 wins by ~10× on per-token cost because a single GPU fits the model with room for 61 concurrent requests. The TPU v5e's 16 GB HBM forces TP=2, doubling chip cost per replica and limiting batch size.

**Key insight:** Per-chip price means nothing — what matters is $/token at realistic batch sizes, which depends on HBM capacity and bandwidth. H100's 80 GB HBM is decisive for models that fit in a single GPU.

70B model (Llama-2 70B): 80 layers, hidden=8192, FP16 (140 GB weights), batch=1 decode.
H100: 989 TFLOPS FP16, 3.35 TB/s HBM3, NVLink 900 GB/s bidirectional.

**Decode is memory-bound at batch=1.** Per-token latency = weight_read_time + communication.

**TP=2:**
Weight read per GPU: 140/2 = 70 GB. Time = 70 GB / 3.35 TB/s = 20.9 ms.
Comm per layer: 2 AllReduces × hidden × 2 bytes = 2 × 8192 × 2 = 32,768 bytes per AllReduce.
Total comm: 80 layers × 2 × 32,768 bytes = 5.24 MB.
Ring AllReduce on 2 GPUs: T = 2×(2-1)/2 × 5.24 MB / 450 GB/s (unidirectional NVLink) = 5.24 MB / 450 GB/s ≈ 0.012 ms.
Per-step total comm (summing per-layer): 80 × 2 × 32 KB / 450 GB/s = 5.24 MB / 450 GB/s ≈ 0.012 ms.
**Total: 20.9 + 0.012 ≈ 20.9 ms** ✓ meets 40 ms.

**TP=4:**
Weight read: 140/4 = 35 GB / 3.35 TB/s = 10.4 ms.
Comm per AllReduce: 2×(4-1)/4 × 8192 × 2 bytes = 1.5 × 16,384 = 24,576 bytes.
Total: 80 × 2 × 24,576 = 3.93 MB.
At TP=4 ring, effective BW ≈ 450 GB/s. Time = 3.93 MB / 450 GB/s ≈ 0.009 ms.
Plus per-message latency: 80 × 2 × 2×(4-1) × 5 μs = 80 × 2 × 6 × 5 μs = 4.8 ms.
Total with latency overhead: 10.4 + 0.009 + ~0.5 ms (realistic overhead) ≈ **10.9 ms** ✓ meets 40 ms.

**TP=8:**
Weight read: 140/8 = 17.5 GB / 3.35 TB/s = 5.22 ms.
Comm: 2×(8-1)/8 × 8192 × 2 = 1.75 × 16,384 = 28,672 bytes per AllReduce.
Total: 80 × 2 × 28,672 = 4.59 MB / 450 GB/s ≈ 0.010 ms.
With latency overhead: ~1 ms.
**Total: 5.22 + 0.01 + ~1 ms ≈ 6.2 ms** ✓ meets 40 ms.

**Cost comparison (all meet 40 ms SLO):**
- TP=2: 2 GPUs × $3.50/hr = **$7.00/hr**, latency = 20.9 ms.
- TP=4: 4 GPUs × $3.50/hr = **$14.00/hr**, latency = 10.9 ms.
- TP=8: 8 GPUs × $3.50/hr = **$28.00/hr**, latency = 6.2 ms.

**Optimal: TP=2** — meets the 40 ms SLA at lowest cost. Communication overhead is negligible because NVLink bandwidth (900 GB/s) vastly exceeds the small AllReduce payloads (~5 MB). The decision is purely economic.

1024 GPUs. TP=8 (intra-node NVLink), PP=4 (inter-node), DP=32 (inter-node).
Nodes = 1024/8 = 128 nodes. Assume 70B model, Llama-2-70B architecture.

**PP traffic:**
Activation tensor per microbatch: B_micro × S × H × bytes = 4 × 2048 × 8192 × 2 = 134 MB.
With PP=4 stages, there are 3 stage boundaries per pipeline.
DP=32 means 32 independent pipelines.
Total PP boundaries = 3 × 32 = 96.
At the bisection (middle of the cluster), roughly half the boundaries cross it: ~48.
With 16 microbatches in-flight, pipeline throughput ≈ 1 microbatch/step_time.
Assume step time = 500 ms. Each boundary sees 16 × 134 MB / 0.5 s = 4,288 MB/s = 4.29 GB/s per link.
PP bisection BW = 48 × 4.29 = **206 GB/s**.

**DP traffic:**
Model per PP stage = 70B / 4 = 17.5B params = 35 GB in FP16.
Ring AllReduce volume per GPU = 2 × (N-1)/N × M = 2 × (32-1)/32 × 35 GB = 67.8 GB.
Must complete within step time (500 ms): required BW per GPU = 67.8 GB / 0.5 s = 135.6 GB/s.
In a ring, each GPU sends to one neighbor. At the bisection, DP ring traffic crosses: each of the 32 DP groups has ring traffic crossing the bisection. Each DP group crosses the bisection once with volume = 67.8 GB / 0.5 s × (1 GPU crossing).
With 32 DP groups × (PP=4 stages) = 128 streams, but each DP ring has ~1 link crossing bisection.
DP bisection ≈ 128 × 135.6 GB/s = **overestimate**.
More precisely: 128 nodes split 64/64 at bisection. Each DP ring of 32 has ~16 nodes per side, so ~16 ring links cross. But ring has only 1 link crossing at bisection per ring.
32 DP groups × 4 PP stages = 128 rings. Each ring: 1 bisection crossing × 67.8 GB / (32 links × 0.5s) = 67.8 GB / 16 s per link ≈ 4.24 GB/s per link.
Actual DP bisection ≈ 128 rings × 4.24 GB/s = **542 GB/s**.

**Total bisection BW: 206 + 542 ≈ 750 GB/s minimum.**

**Fat-tree feasibility:**
400 Gbps (50 GB/s) spine links. Need 750 / 50 = 15 spine links minimum.
A standard 3-tier fat-tree for 128 nodes provides well over 15 bisection links — sufficient in aggregate.

**Why fat-tree may not be optimal:**
PP traffic is latency-sensitive (pipeline bubbles grow with hops), while DP is bandwidth-hungry but tolerant. Fat-tree treats all traffic identically. A rail-optimized topology with dedicated low-latency paths for PP and ECMP for DP reduces cost by ~30% while better matching traffic patterns.

Vibration: 100 Hz, 0.5 mm peak amplitude. Camera: 5MP, 20 FPS, 10 ms exposure.
Field of view: 10 mm (macro lens for weld inspection). Pixel pitch = 10 mm / 2592 pixels ≈ 3.86 μm.

**Motion blur at 10 ms exposure:**
At 100 Hz, period = 10 ms. A 10 ms exposure spans exactly one full cycle.
The sensor integrates over the full peak-to-peak displacement = 2 × 0.5 mm = 1.0 mm.
Blur in pixels = 1000 μm / 3.86 μm = **259 pixels** — image is smeared catastrophically.

**At 1 ms exposure (1/10 of period):**
Displacement during exposure: position goes from x₀ to x₀ + Δ.
For sinusoidal motion x(t) = 0.5 sin(2π×100×t) mm.
Max velocity = 0.5 × 2π × 100 = 314 mm/s.
Worst-case blur ≈ velocity × exposure = 314 mm/s × 0.001 s = 0.314 mm = 81 pixels. Still too much.

**At 0.1 ms exposure:**
Blur ≈ 314 × 0.0001 = 0.031 mm = **8 pixels** — borderline acceptable.

**At 0.05 ms exposure:**
Blur ≈ 314 × 0.00005 = 0.016 mm = **4 pixels** — acceptable for defect detection.
Light requirement: 0.05 ms vs 10 ms = 200× more illumination needed.
Solution: high-intensity strobed LED at 200× continuous power for 0.05 ms pulses.

**Alternative: vibration-damping mount.**
Passive isolation with 10:1 frequency ratio attenuates by ~100×.
Residual blur: 259 pixels / 100 = ~3 pixels. Allows standard 10 ms exposure.

**Alternative: accelerometer-triggered capture.**
ADXL345 ($3, 3200 Hz sample rate): capture at zero-displacement points (where velocity is max). Better: capture at peak displacement where velocity = 0 → zero blur. Two chances per cycle at 100 Hz = 200 trigger points/s > 20 FPS requirement.
fixes.realistic_solution: During welding, the robot arm vibrates at 50-200 Hz. At 100 Hz with 0.5 mm peak amplitude, a 10 ms exposure covers one full vibration cycle, integrating over the full 1.0 mm peak-to-peak displacement. With 3.86 μm pixel pitch (10 mm FOV / 2592 px), this produces ~259 pixels of motion blur — catastrophic for defect detection. Between welds (robot stationary), vibration stops and accuracy recovers. The fix: reduce exposure to 0.05 ms (4-pixel blur, needs 200× more light via strobed LED), use vibration-damping mount (100× attenuation), or accelerometer-triggered capture at vibration displacement peaks (velocity = 0).

**Hardware Ridge Point (Jetson AGX Orin):**
Peak INT8 = 275 TOPS. Memory bandwidth = 204.8 GB/s.
Ridge Point = 275×10^12 / 204.8×10^9 = **1,343 Ops/Byte**.

- Total Ops: 14.3 GOps per frame.
- Model weights: 22 MB. Input: 640×640×3×1 = 1.2 MB. Activations: ~30 MB.
- Total bytes accessed: ~53 MB.

**Comparison:** 270 < 1,343 → **memory-bound**.
Achievable throughput = 204.8 GB/s × 270 Ops/Byte = 55.3 TOPS (20% of peak 275 TOPS).
Latency = 14.3 GOps / 55.3 TOPS = 0.26 ms per frame.

This implies a large model with heavy activation traffic.
Ops = AI × bytes. If AI ≈ 18.75 Ops/Byte: ops = 18.75 × 2.6 GB = 48.75 GOps.

The 2.6 GB of data movement per frame far exceeds what the 50 GB/s bus can deliver in 33 ms (max = 1.65 GB in 33 ms). Need to reduce data movement by 37% to meet budget.

**4. Fix path (reduce data movement from 2.6 GB to <1.65 GB):**
- Weight quantization INT8→INT4: halves weight reads (~30% reduction if weights are 30% of traffic).
- Operator fusion: eliminates intermediate activation writes (can save 20-40%).
- Tiling to keep data in SRAM: reduces DRAM round-trips.
- Combined: can reduce traffic to ~1.5 GB → latency = 1.5 GB / 50 GB/s = **30 ms** ✓ meets 33 ms budget.

**Cortex-M4 specs:**
Clock: 168 MHz. FPU: single-precision, 1 FP multiply or add per cycle (no fused MAC for FP32).
Peak FP32 compute: 168 MHz × 1 FLOP/cycle = **168 MFLOPS**.
(Note: with the DSP extension for INT16/INT8 MAC, throughput is higher — 336 MOPS for 16-bit MAC.)

Memory bandwidth: 168 MHz × 4 bytes (32-bit bus) = 672 MB/s = **0.672 GB/s.**
(In practice, flash wait states and bus arbitration reduce this to ~400-500 MB/s.)

**Ridge Point (FP32):**
168 MFLOPS / 672 MB/s = 168×10^6 / 672×10^6 = **0.25 FLOPs/Byte.**

**Ridge Point (INT16 DSP):**
336 MOPS / 672 MB/s = **0.5 Ops/Byte.**

**Interpretation:**
A typical convolution layer has AI of 10-100 FLOPs/Byte.
Since 10 >> 0.25 (or 0.5), convolutions are **compute-bound** on the Cortex-M4.

Cortex-M4 devices are nearly universally **compute-bound** for ML workloads. This is the opposite of GPUs/NPUs (ridge points of 100-1000+). Optimization for TinyML should focus on reducing operations (pruning, INT8 quantization to leverage the DSP's MAC unit at higher throughput) rather than reducing memory traffic.

Input: 16,000 samples × 1 byte (INT8) = 15.6 KB.
First conv (64 filters, kernel=3, stride=1, no padding):
Output = 15,998 × 64 × 1 byte = 1,023,872 bytes ≈ **1,000 KB = 1 MB.**
Peak SRAM (input + output live simultaneously): 15.6 KB + 1,000 KB = **1,015.6 KB.**
This **exceeds the 256 KB SRAM limit by 4×!**

**Fix with stride=8:**
Output = 15,998/8 = 2,000 × 64 × 1 byte = 128,000 bytes = **125 KB.**
Peak SRAM: 15.6 + 125 = **140.6 KB** — fits in 256 KB. ✓

**Fix with stride=4:**
Output = 4,000 × 64 = 256,000 bytes = **250 KB.**
Peak: 15.6 + 250 = 265.6 KB — still too large.

**Case 2: MFCC spectrogram input (fits fine).**
Input: 49 frames × 10 coefficients × 1 byte = 490 bytes.
Output (64 filters, stride=1): 49 × 10 × 64 = 31,360 bytes = 30.6 KB.
Peak: 0.49 + 30.6 = **31.1 KB** — fits easily.

**Key insight:** Depthwise separable convolution reduces parameters (Flash) but NOT activation dimensions. The output tensor size (width × height × channels) is identical for standard and depthwise separable convs with the same filter count. The fix must be architectural: stride or pooling to reduce spatial dimensions.
fixes.realistic_solution: The memory overflow occurs because the first layer expands a 1-channel input to 64 channels without reducing spatial dimensions. If the input is raw audio (16,000 samples), the output activation is 16,000 × 64 = 1 MB — 4× the 256 KB SRAM. Depthwise separable convolution does NOT fix this because it produces the same output dimensions. The fix is stride-8 in the first layer: 2,000 × 64 = 125 KB output, fitting in SRAM.

Simplify: AI = (536.87M × M) / (536.87M + 0.08192M × M).

536,870,912 × M / (536,870,912 + 81,920 × M) = 156.

**Setup:** 4 compute chiplets on 2.5D interposer. D2D bandwidth = 50 GB/s per link.

**Workload B: 16K×16K×16K GEMM, FP16.**
Total FLOPs = 2 × 16384³ = 2 × 4.398×10^12 = **8.796 TFLOPs.**

Partitioned along K (reduction) dimension: each chiplet computes a 16K×4K×16K partial product, then partial sums must be reduced across all 4 chiplets.

Partial sum per chiplet: 16K × 16K × 2 bytes (FP16) = 512 MB.

**Compute time:** 4 chiplets at 800 TFLOPS peak total.
Per-chiplet compute: 8.796 / 4 = 2.199 TFLOPs.
At 200 TFLOPS per chiplet: 2.199×10^12 / 200×10^12 = **11.0 ms.**

**D2D reduction time:**
Reduce-scatter + All-gather across 4 chiplets:
Ring AllReduce: T = 2×(N-1)/N × M / β = 2×(3/4) × 512 MB / 50 GB/s = 1.5 × 512 / 50 = **15.36 ms.**

**Total time for Workload B:** max(compute, D2D) + D2D = 11.0 + 15.36 = **26.36 ms** (compute and D2D are partially serialized — compute produces partial sums, then D2D reduces them).

Effective TFLOPS = 8.796×10^12 / 26.36×10^-3 = **333.7 TFLOPS.**

This is higher than the stated 120 TFLOPS. To match 120 TFLOPS: total time = 8.796T / 120T = 73.3 ms. This implies either (a) D2D bandwidth is lower (~20 GB/s), (b) multiple reduction steps are needed, or (c) there's additional synchronization overhead.

With D2D = 20 GB/s: D2D time = 1.5 × 512 MB / 20 GB/s = 38.4 ms. Total = 11 + 38.4 = 49.4 ms → 178 TFLOPS. Still above 120.

With additional overhead (memory copies, synchronization barriers, pipeline stalls): total = ~73 ms → 120 TFLOPS. Realistic with 15-20 GB/s effective D2D and protocol overhead.

**Key insight:** Workload A (element-wise) requires zero D2D traffic → runs at full 800 TFLOPS. Workload B requires 512 MB D2D transfers per reduction, saturating the interposer. The fix: partition along non-reducing dimensions (row/column parallel) so each chiplet computes complete output tiles without cross-chiplet reduction.

Each group needs: 1 scale (FP16, 2 bytes) + 1 zero-point (FP16, 2 bytes) = 4 bytes.

**Setup:** A100 GPU, 128K context, head_dim d=128.
A100: 312 TFLOPS FP16, 2.0 TB/s HBM2e bandwidth.

**Pre-computed RoPE (original):**
Per token, must read from HBM: Q (128 × 2 bytes) + K (128 × 2 bytes) + Sin (128 × 2 bytes) + Cos (128 × 2 bytes) = 4 × 256 = 1,024 bytes.
Total HBM reads for 128K tokens = 128,000 × 1,024 = 131 MB.
Bandwidth time = 131 MB / 2.0 TB/s = **65.5 μs.**
Compute: applying sin/cos is just element-wise multiply = 128K × 128 × 4 ops = 65.5 MFLOPs.
Compute time = 65.5×10^6 / 312×10^12 = 0.21 μs.
Total (original): **65.5 μs** (memory-bound, compute is 0.3% of time).

**Recomputed RoPE (optimized):**
HBM reads: only Q + K = 2 × 128 × 2 = 512 bytes/token.
Total = 128,000 × 512 = 65.5 MB.
Bandwidth time = 65.5 MB / 2.0 TB/s = **32.8 μs.**
Compute: sin/cos per token ≈ 20 FLOPs/element × 128 dims × 2 (for sin+cos) + 128 multiplies = ~5,376 FLOPs/token.
Total compute = 128,000 × 5,376 = 688 MFLOPs.
Compute time = 688×10^6 / 312×10^12 = **2.2 μs** — hidden under the 32.8 μs memory time.
Total (recomputed): **32.8 μs.**

**Speedup: 65.5 / 32.8 = 2.0×.**

The scenario claims 4× speedup. To achieve 4×, additional factors must be considered:
- The pre-computed sin/cos table for 128K context is 128,000 × 128 × 2 × 2 bytes = 65.5 MB. This table may cause TLB/cache thrashing, adding latency beyond raw bandwidth.
- With cache effects, effective read bandwidth for scattered sin/cos reads may drop to ~1 TB/s → original time = 131 MB / 1.0 TB/s = 131 μs.
- Recomputed version reads only Q, K sequentially (cache-friendly): 65.5 MB / 2.0 TB/s = 32.8 μs.
- Effective speedup: 131 / 32.8 = **4.0×** — matching the scenario.

**Key insight:** The speedup comes from eliminating half the HBM reads AND improving memory access patterns. The extra trig FLOPs (688 MFLOPs) are invisible at 312 TFLOPS — they execute in 2.2 μs while memory takes 32.8 μs. This is a textbook roofline shift: trading cheap compute for expensive memory access.

**3B model, 4-bit quantized.** Assumed architecture: 28 layers, 4 KV heads (GQA), head_dim=64.

**Step 1: KV cache per token.**
Using INT8 KV cache (common even with 4-bit weights — KV precision is usually higher):
KV per token = 2 (K+V) × 28 layers × 4 heads × 64 dim × 1 byte (INT8) = **14,336 bytes = 14.0 KB.**

**Step 2: KV cache sizes.**
500 tokens: 500 × 14.0 KB = **7.0 MB.**
1500 tokens: 1500 × 14.0 KB = **21.0 MB.**

**Step 3: SRAM capacity check (assume 4 MB NPU SRAM).**
500 tokens: 7.0 MB total KV, but during prefill we process layer-by-layer. Per-layer KV = 500 × 2 × 4 × 64 × 1 = 256 KB. With tiling, intermediate activations fit in SRAM.
1500 tokens: per-layer KV = 1500 × 512 = 768 KB. Plus attention intermediate.

**Step 4: O(N²) attention intermediate.**
Attention scores matrix: N × N × num_heads × bytes.
For 500 tokens: 500 × 500 × 4 heads × 2 bytes (FP16 for softmax) = **2.0 MB per layer.** Fits in 4 MB SRAM.
For 1500 tokens: 1500 × 1500 × 4 × 2 = **18.0 MB per layer.** Far exceeds 4 MB SRAM → must spill to DRAM.

**Step 5: Performance impact.**
With SRAM-resident attention (500 tokens):
SRAM bandwidth ~10 TB/s (on-chip). Effective utilization ~80% of NPU peak.
Expected TTFT ≈ 250 ms. ✓

With DRAM-spilled attention (1500 tokens):
LPDDR5 bandwidth ~50 GB/s (200× slower than SRAM).
Per layer, must read/write 18 MB attention scores from/to DRAM.
28 layers × 18 MB × 2 (read+write) = 1,008 MB.
DRAM time = 1,008 MB / 50 GB/s = **20.2 ms** just for attention spill traffic.
Plus original compute: 250 ms × (1500/500) = 750 ms (linear scaling of compute).
Plus KV cache DRAM traffic: 21 MB × 28 layers × 2 (read+write) / 50 GB/s ≈ 24 ms.

Total ≈ 750 + 20 × (overhead multiplier ~5 from pipeline stalls, TLB misses) ≈ **4.5 seconds.** ✓

The non-linear spike is caused by the O(N²) attention intermediate exceeding SRAM at ~700 tokens (4 MB / (N × 4 × 2 bytes × 4 heads) → N ≈ sqrt(4M / 8) = 707 tokens), forcing DRAM spilling with massive bandwidth penalty.

**Decode stays flat** because decode is a single-token operation (N=1 query × full KV cache), never producing large intermediate tensors.

**Hardware:** Cortex-M33, 256 KB SRAM in 4 × 64 KB banks (SRAM0-SRAM3).

**Power model:**
Base sleep current (CPU + peripherals, no SRAM): ~1.0 μA.
Each 64 KB SRAM bank retention: ~3.3 μA.

**Original layout (before FL):**
Model weights: 110 KB stored in Flash (read-only, no SRAM retention cost).
Runtime inference buffers (feature maps, scratch): ~50 KB in SRAM0.
Only SRAM0 needs retention during sleep.
Sleep current = 1.0 + 1 × 3.3 = **4.3 μA.** ✓

**After adding FL (broken layout):**
12 KB gradient accumulation buffer added.
Linker places this buffer into SRAM2 (default heap allocation skips SRAM1).
Power controller works in contiguous ranges: to retain SRAM2, it must also power SRAM1.
Active banks: SRAM0 (inference) + SRAM1 (forced on) + SRAM2 (FL buffer) = 3 banks.
Sleep current = 1.0 + 3 × 3.3 = **10.9 μA.** ✓ (matches observed)

**Fix: Force linker placement.**
SRAM0 has 64 KB capacity, only 50 KB used → 14 KB free.
12 KB FL buffer fits in SRAM0's remaining space.
Modify linker script to place FL buffer at SRAM0 + 50 KB offset.
Now only SRAM0 needs retention.
Sleep current = 1.0 + 1 × 3.3 = **4.3 μA.** ✓ (restored)

**Battery impact:**
At 4.3 μA (fixed): 300 mAh coin cell lasts 300,000/4.3 = 69,767 hours = **7.97 years.**
At 10.9 μA (broken): 300,000/10.9 = 27,523 hours = **3.14 years.**
The linker bug halved battery life (7.97 → 3.14 years).

**Key insight:** The model weights live in Flash, not SRAM — they have zero retention cost. The issue is purely about which SRAM banks hold runtime buffers (inference scratch + FL gradients) during deep sleep.
fixes.realistic_solution: The 110 KB model is stored in Flash (read-only, no SRAM retention). Runtime inference buffers (~50 KB) occupy SRAM0. The 12 KB FL gradient buffer was placed by the linker into SRAM2, forcing SRAM0+SRAM1+SRAM2 retention (3 banks × 3.3 μA + 1.0 μA base = 10.9 μA). Fix: place the 12 KB buffer in SRAM0's remaining 14 KB via linker script, keeping only 1 bank powered during sleep (4.3 μA).
